---
title: "Project Brief for Future Civic Document Shiny Apps"
format:
  html: default
  typst: default
---

Successfully deployed to <https://danswart.shinyapps.io/schertz-council-docs/>

2026-04-15



# Purpose of this document

This document is written for the assistant, not for the end user.

Its purpose is to preserve the key lessons learned while building the Schertz City Council document-search Shiny prototype so that future similar projects can start from the right mental model and avoid rediscovering the same methods, pitfalls, and architecture decisions.

This is intended to be fed to the assistant at the start of future similar projects.

Use this document as:

- an implementation playbook
- an architecture guide
- a debugging checklist
- a startup research plan
- a record of what worked and what failed

Use it for projects involving:

- municipal agendas
- minutes
- archived public records
- civic-facing Shiny apps
- Laserfiche
- Destiny Hosted
- Granicus
- public document portals
- searchable local-government archives

# Project philosophy

## 1. The app should be thin.

The Shiny app should read finished local files only.

The app should not:

- scrape websites live during ordinary user interaction
- discover new records on startup
- call archive portals inside reactive search logic
- depend on public portals being stable

The app should:

- load local `.rds` and `.csv` files
- rebuild `search_text` from current fields
- provide filters and search only
- expose links to records or PDFs
- remain fast and predictable

## 2. The pipeline should do the hard work.

The correct order is:

1. discover records
2. normalize metadata
3. download or retrieve content
4. extract text
5. merge and deduplicate
6. write final local files
7. let Shiny read those files

# Preferred architecture

## 3. Use modular source-specific scripts.

Never try to solve all source systems in one giant script.

Create one module per source or source type.

Preferred pattern:

- `R/00_config.R`
- `R/run_pipeline.R`
- `R/normalize_documents.R`
- `R/download_documents.R`
- `R/extract_text.R`
- `R/build_search_data.R`

Source-specific modules:

- `R/discover_destinyhosted_agendas.R`
- `R/discover_destinyhosted_minutes.R`
- `R/discover_laserfiche_minutes.R`
- later:
  - `R/discover_boards_commissions.R`
  - `R/discover_city_calendar.R`
  - `R/discover_granicus.R`

Source-specific text modules when needed:

- `R/extract_laserfiche_minutes_text.R`
- later, if needed:
  - `R/extract_granicus_text.R`
  - `R/extract_special_archive_text.R`

## 4. Normalize all modules to a common schema.

Every discovery module should output a tibble with the same fields, or fields that can easily be transformed into the same structure.

Preferred schema:

- `doc_id`
- `source_system`
- `source_category`
- `meeting_body`
- `meeting_type`
- `meeting_date`
- `fiscal_year`
- `doc_type`
- `title`
- `source_page_url`
- `document_url`
- `pdf_url`
- `source_record_id`
- `file_name`
- `local_file`
- `text_extracted`
- `text_available`
- `n_pages`
- `discovered_at`
- `downloaded_at`
- `text_extracted_at`
- `is_active`
- `notes`

Then merge and deduplicate in separate steps.

# First questions to answer on any new project

## 5. At the start of a new civic document project, determine these things immediately.

### Source-system questions
- What system is hosting the records?
- Is it one system or several?
- Are current records stored differently from historical records?
- Are agendas and minutes in the same system or different systems?

### Retrieval questions
- Does the system expose direct PDF links?
- Is the visible page ordinary HTML or a JavaScript shell?
- Are there internal service endpoints?
- Is there a “View Text” or “Plain Text” mode?
- Is there an RSS feed?
- Is there a JSON API?
- Are there cookies or session requirements?

### Document-type questions
- Are files born-digital PDFs?
- Are some rows only metadata records?
- Are some old records scans or image-based objects?
- Is there a signal like `isEdoc = TRUE` that identifies good candidates?

### Merge questions
- Which source should be preferred when overlap exists?
- Do recent records overlap with archive records?
- What is the deduplication key?
- Is `meeting_date` enough, or must title and type be included too?

### Search questions
- Which rows will be truly full-text searchable?
- Which rows will only be metadata searchable?
- How will the UI represent this honestly?

# Start-up workflow for a new portal

## 6. The first ten technical things to test on a new portal

1. Open the human-facing page in a browser and identify the source system.
2. Test whether `rvest::read_html()` sees real rows or only a JS shell.
3. Look for a service bundle or JS app file.
4. Inspect JS for service endpoints.
5. Test one discovery endpoint manually.
6. Test one metadata endpoint manually.
7. Test one document info endpoint manually.
8. Test whether a plain-text or text-view endpoint exists.
9. Test whether direct PDF/open behavior exists.
10. Save one success path before attempting generalization.

Do not generalize too early.

# Lessons about specific systems

## 7. Destiny Hosted lessons

Destiny Hosted was comparatively cooperative.

Useful properties:

- current records were easier to discover
- document links were more straightforward
- recent agenda packets and minutes were retrievable
- extraction with `pdftools` worked well for many records

Practical lesson:

Use Destiny Hosted for the easy-wins phase first.

If there is overlap with archives, let Destiny Hosted usually win for recent/current records.

## 8. Laserfiche lessons

Laserfiche was much more complicated, but also much more powerful once understood.

### 8a. Visible HTML is often misleading
Reading `Browse.aspx` or `DocView.aspx` with `rvest::read_html()` often returns only:

- loading text
- JavaScript bootstrap code
- viewer scaffolding
- no usable rows
- no real document text

This does not mean the data is unavailable.

### 8b. Internal service endpoints are the real source of truth
Important endpoints discovered:

Folder and metadata side:

- `FolderListingService.aspx/GetFolderListing2`
- `FolderListingService.aspx/GetFolderListingIds`
- `FolderListingService.aspx/GetBreadCrumbs`
- `FolderListingService.aspx/GetDocumentInfo`

Text side:

- `DocumentService.aspx/GetTextHtmlForPage`

This last endpoint was the critical breakthrough.

### 8c. `DocView.aspx` body text is not the document text
Scraping `DocView.aspx` body produced shell text like:

- `Loading...`
- viewer labels
- JavaScript

That created false positives.

Important rule:

Do not treat `DocView.aspx` body text as extracted document text.

### 8d. The “View Text” toggle matters
If the user reports a “View Text” or “View Plain Text” toggle in the portal, assume there is an underlying service endpoint for text retrieval.

Do not pivot to OCR too quickly.

### 8e. `isEdoc = TRUE` is the key signal
In Schertz, the correct strategic boundary was:

- include `isEdoc = TRUE`
- exclude `isEdoc = FALSE` from full-text ambitions

That was the right choice.

### 8f. Session handling matters
Laserfiche behaved like a session-limited or cookie-dependent system.

Correct mitigation:

- warm a session first
- preserve cookies with a file
- use a stable user agent
- add sleeps
- add retries with backoff
- do not hammer the service

### 8g. Do not use Laserfiche inside live app logic
Laserfiche belongs in preprocessing only.

# Rules for deciding what to include

## 9. What to include by default

Include by default:

- directly downloadable born-digital PDFs
- recent current-system records
- archive records with strong evidence of reliable text retrieval
- `isEdoc = TRUE` records when there is a text service

## 10. What to exclude by default

Exclude by default unless the user explicitly wants them:

- image-only records
- scanned documents requiring OCR
- unstable session-dependent live retrieval in the app
- archive records with no reliable text path
- records that require heroic handling but add little value

## 11. OCR default rule

Default rule:

- do not pursue OCR unless the user clearly wants it

Reason:

- low quality
- high complexity
- noisy search results
- fragile pipeline
- often not worth it for civic documents if a cleaner subset is available

# Data-processing lessons

## 12. Save intermediate outputs at every major stage

Always save:

- raw discovery output
- normalized metadata
- post-download output
- post-text-extraction output
- final merged dataset

Helpful files include:

- `*_documents.rds`
- `*_documents.csv`
- `*_raw_rows.rds`
- `*_raw_rows.csv`
- `*_with_text.rds`
- final `documents.rds`

## 13. The app should never be the first place a data problem is discovered

Validate in R first:

- counts by type
- counts by `text_available`
- overlap by date
- sample rows
- direct `grepl()` on known names

Only then test the app.

## 14. Replace stale rows with enriched rows

If a source is first merged without text and later enriched with text, do not just append the new rows and anti-join by date.

Correct pattern:

- drop old rows from that source/type
- bind the enriched rows
- rebuild final search data

This was critical for Laserfiche minutes.

## 15. Rebuild `search_text` every time the app loads

Do not trust stored `search_text`.

Always rebuild it from:

- `title`
- `meeting_type`
- `doc_type`
- `text_extracted`

This avoids stale-index bugs.

# Search validation lessons

## 16. “Has Text” can lie if the wrong text source was captured

A row may show `Has Text = Yes` while containing only:

- shell text
- viewer text
- labels
- portal chrome

Therefore, “Has Text” should really mean:

- has extracted usable document text

Use direct row inspection when suspicious.

## 17. Validate search with hard cases, not easy cases

Good tests:

- a council member’s name
- a mayor’s name
- a distinctive place name
- a known phrase from a historical minute
- a person whose name should appear across many years

Weak tests:

- budget
- vote
- meeting
- minutes

Generic words can match metadata or titles and are not strong proof of true full-text search.

## 18. Inspect the exact row when in doubt

Use direct row inspection:

- `nchar(text_extracted)`
- `grepl("name", text_extracted, ignore.case = TRUE)`
- inspect the actual stored text

This quickly tells whether the problem is:

- extraction source
- stale search index
- merge bug
- app bug

# Debugging workflow that worked best

## 19. Always narrow the scope

When something seems wrong:

1. identify one known record
2. inspect its row
3. inspect the stored text length
4. test exact term presence in that row
5. test one service call manually
6. only then generalize

This worked much better than broad speculative debugging.

## 20. Use manual service-call probes before rewriting modules

For example:

- call `GetFolderListing2` manually
- inspect the JSON
- call `GetDocumentInfo`
- inspect page count and `isEdoc`
- call `GetTextHtmlForPage` for page 1
- confirm real text
- then rewrite extraction module

That order is safer than guessing.

## 21. Interpret server errors cautiously

HTTP 500 does not necessarily mean:

- your code is wrong
- the endpoint is invalid

It may mean:

- session issue
- cookie issue
- rate limit
- licensing/session cap
- transient portal instability

Never overinterpret a single 500.

# Preferred module behavior

## 22. Discovery modules should

- be source-specific
- return normalized metadata rows
- avoid doing too much at once
- save intermediate outputs
- record notes about assumptions

## 23. Text modules should

- accept the discovered metadata rows
- enrich only relevant records
- update `text_extracted`
- update `text_available`
- update `text_extracted_at`
- save enriched outputs

## 24. Build-search module should

- rebuild `search_text`
- build links appropriate for source type
- save final `documents.rds`
- save final `documents.csv`

## 25. App should

- load local files only
- rebuild `search_text`
- expose filters
- expose “Has Text”
- expose source label
- never touch live portals

# Recommended file/module skeleton

## 26. Baseline project structure

```text
project/
  app.R
  README_start_here.qmd
  R/
    00_config.R
    run_pipeline.R
    normalize_documents.R
    build_search_data.R
    download_documents.R
    extract_text.R
    discover_destinyhosted_agendas.R
    discover_destinyhosted_minutes.R
    discover_laserfiche_minutes.R
    extract_laserfiche_minutes_text.R
  data/
    raw/
    derived/
  cache/
  logs/
```

## 27. Good config values to expose

`00_config.R` should centralize:

- user agent
- request timeout
- sleep seconds
- folder paths
- meeting body
- derived dir
- raw dir
- cache dir
- log dir

# Important merge and deduplication rules

## 28. Deduplicate after both streams are assembled

For minutes, preferred deduplication key was primarily:

- `meeting_body`
- `meeting_date`
- `doc_type`

Then prefer source priority.

## 29. Use source priority consciously

For Schertz:

- keep current source for overlapping recent minutes
- use Laserfiche as historical backfill

Do not let archive rows overwrite good current rows unnecessarily.

# User-facing honesty rules

## 30. Never overpromise corpus coverage

A good civic-document app should be honest about:

- which years are covered
- which records are full-text searchable
- which records are only metadata searchable
- which records were excluded

## 31. Good coverage-note language

Useful phrasing:

- “This app indexes electronically retrievable records.”
- “Historical archive coverage has been expanded where reliable text extraction was possible.”
- “Some older image-style archival records are not yet included in full-text search.”

# What worked especially well in the Schertz project

## 32. The winning combination

The project succeeded because of this combination:

- thin app
- heavy preprocessing pipeline
- source-specific modules
- saving intermediate outputs
- preferring current system for recent rows
- using Laserfiche service endpoints
- filtering to `isEdoc = TRUE`
- using `GetTextHtmlForPage`
- rebuilding `search_text`
- validating with real names and historical records

This should be the default blueprint for similar projects.

# Mistakes to avoid next time

## 33. Avoid these mistakes

- putting live scraping inside app logic
- trusting `DocView.aspx` body text
- assuming visible HTML contains real content
- assuming `Has Text = Yes` means useful text
- trusting stored `search_text`
- trying to solve all sources in one function
- pursuing OCR when the user does not want it
- concluding too quickly that a portal has no usable text source
- assuming 500 errors always mean code failure

# Operational checklist for future projects

## 34. At the start of the next similar project, do this in order

1. identify source systems
2. decide which source gets a separate module
3. test whether visible HTML is real or JS shell
4. search JS for internal service endpoints
5. identify metadata/folder endpoints
6. identify document info endpoint
7. identify text endpoint
8. determine whether an `isEdoc`-like signal exists
9. define inclusion/exclusion rules
10. build thin app only after the pipeline works

# Final instruction to future assistants

## 35. Default assumptions

When starting a similar project, assume the following unless evidence says otherwise:

- the app should be thin
- the pipeline should do the hard work
- sources should be modular
- archive viewers may require internal service calls
- usable historical full-text is more likely to come from text endpoints than from page scraping
- `isEdoc = TRUE` is a strong signal to prioritize a record
- non-edoc records should usually be excluded from full-text scope unless explicitly required
- stale `search_text` can create false app failures
- the right debugging move is usually to inspect one row and one service call, not to speculate broadly

If this document is available at the start of a new project, use it as the implementation playbook.
